author: Augustine of Hippo

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Having manifested what he was and what he is, he shows the great fruit of his confession; and being about to examine by what method God and the happy life may be found, he enlarges on the nature and power of memory. Then he examines his own acts, thoughts and affections, viewed under the threefold division of temptation; and commemorates the Lord, the one mediator of God and men.
Of the Charms of Perfumes Which are More Easily Overcome.

ccel.title: NPNF1-01. The Confessions and Letters of St. Augustine, with a Sketch of his Life and Work

source.title: NPNF1-01. The Confessions and Letters of St. Augustine, with a Sketch of his Life and Work

originalBook: Having manifested what he was and what he is, he shows the great fruit of his confession; and being about to examine by what method God and the happy life may be found, he enlarges on the nature and power of memory. Then he examines his own acts, thoughts and affections, viewed under the threefold division of temptation; and commemorates the Lord, the one mediator of God and men.
title: Of the Charms of Perfumes Which are More Easily Overcome.

source.title: NPNF1-01. The Confessions and Letters of St. Augustine, with a Sketch of his Life and Work

verses.text: With the attractions of odours I am not much troubled.
When absent I do not seek them; when present I do not refuse them; and am prepared ever to be without them.
At any rate thus I appear to myself; perchance I am deceived.

For that also is a lamentable darkness wherein my capacity that is in me is concealed, so that my mind, making inquiry into herself concerning her own powers, ventures not readily to credit herself; because that which is already in it is, for the most part, concealed, unless experience reveal it.
And no man ought to feel secure in this life, the whole of which is called a temptation, that he, who could be made better from worse, may not also from better be made worse.
Our sole hope, our sole confidence, our sole assured promise, is Thy mercy.
